content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance
(916) 322-5662

Administration
322-5660

Executive/Legal
322-3901

Enforcement
322-6441

Statements of Economic Interest
322-6444

October 21, 1982


Jonathan Lewis
J. Lewis Associates, Inc.
1010 Eleventh Street, Ste. 300
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Jonathan:

     This is in reply to your letter dated August 27, 1982,
regarding the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act.

     You described a situation in which J. Lewis Associates, Inc.
may be involved in arranging and coordinating an event for one of
its clients for the purpose of bringing together selected
corporate governmental relations staff and key government
officials to discuss wide-ranging public policy issues. You
stated that, as a matter of policy, lobbying is prohibited at
these events, and that legislation may be discussed only
tangentially, in the context of illustrating a point or
buttressing an argument.

     You asked whether the discussions occurring at these events
would constitute "direct communication," and whether, because of
the participation of J. Lewis Associates' employees, J. Lewis
Associates or its employees would be engaged in "direct
communication."

     The "roundtable" meetings you described, where the purpose is
an exchange of information, and no pending, proposed or
anticipated legislation is discussed, would not meet the
definition of "direct communication for the purpose of
influencing or attempting to influence legislative action," and
therefore the time spent at the event would not count toward the
"10 hours of direct communication" set out in 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18239(e)(2)(A).

     "Direct communication" is defined in Commission regulation 2
Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239(b) as follows:

Jonathan Lewis
Page 2


"...to talk (either on the telephone or in
person) with any elective state official,
legislative official, or agency official, to
correspond with such officials or to answer
questions or inquiries from such officials,
regardless of whether the communication is in
person or through an agent."

The tests contained in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239,
specifically the "time test" in subsection (e) (2)(A)1, set out
the criteria under which a person will be required to register as
a lobbyist. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239(e)(2)(A)1 provides
that a person "spending a total of 40 hours, including 10 hours
in direct communication, influencing or attempting to influence
legislative or administrative action;..." has met the
"substantial or regular" test set out in the definition of
"lobbyist" in Gov. Code Section 82039.

Therefore, if, as a result of the exchange and sharing of
information at the roundtable meeting, it became clear that some
legislation would be proposed, the time spent at the event would
count as direct communication. And, if an employee of D. Lewis
Associates were to participate in the discussion, the time spent
in direct communication would accrue to the employee. Under
these same circumstances, if the J. Lewis Associates employee's
only involvement in the event were to arrange and coordinate the
event (acting as the client's agent), and he or she did not
participate in the discussions, the time spent in direct
communication would not accrue to the employee, but would accrue
only to the client. The employee's time spent in arranging and
preparing for the roundtable discussion would constitute
non-direct communication and would count toward the 40 hours of
"influencing or attempting to influence" set out in 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18239(e)(2)(A)1.

In addition, in the case where legislation was proposed as a
result of the roundtable discussions, any payments made by the
client in connection with the event would constitute "payments to
influence" and would be reportable pursuant to Gov. Code Section
86108(b) if payments totaling $2,500 or more in a calendar
quarter were made.

I hope this has adequately answered your questions. If you
have any additional questions, please contact us.

Sincerely,

Jeanne Pritchard
Political Reform Consultant

F 22205

J. LEWIS ASSOCIATES, INC.

consulting on the ways and means

Jonathan C. Lewis
David Nagler


August 27, 1982


Jeanne Pritchard
Consultant
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K St.
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ms. Pritchard:

I am writing to request advice from the Fair Political Practices
Commission.

J.LEWIS ASSOCIATES, Inc. is retained by a non-profit, non-partisan
organization dedicated to promoting candid, informative and topical
dialogue between corporate and governmental decisionmakers. This
organization does not lobby or even take positions on legislative or
administrative actions. However, the organization does operate a program in
which selected corporate governmental relations staff (some of whom are
registered lobbyists; many of whom are not) and key government officials
are invited to participate in wide-ranging discussions about important
public policy issues. Group discussions typically consist of one or two
government experts (including elected state officials, legislative
officials and agency officials) and three or four corporate
representatives. As a matter of policy, lobbying is prohibited.
Legislation is discussed at these "roundtable" meetings only tangentially,
that is, in the context of illustrating a point or buttressing an
argument.

What would constitute direct communication at these meetings?
Specifically, since the client does not adopt any positions affecting
state legislative or administrative actions, is it conceivable that
J.LEWIS ASSOCIATES, Inc. or its employees, who are retained
exclusively to arrange and coordinate the event, could possibly be
considered to be engaged in direct communication? If so, under what
circumstances would this occur?

Thank you.

Sincerely,


Jonathan Lewis
                                    association management public affairs consultants
                        1010 Eleventh Street, Suite 300, Sacramento, CA 95814 (916) 442-0238

Jeanne Pritchard

J. LEVVIS ASSOCIATES, INC, 
consulting on the ways and means 
Jonathan C. Lewis

sections.conclusion: The 'roundtable' meetings described, where the purpose is an exchange of information and no pending, proposed or anticipated legislation is discussed, would not meet the definition of 'direct communication for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence legislative action.' However, if legislation would be proposed as a result of the roundtable discussion, the time spent would count as direct communication. If a J. Lewis Associates employee participated in discussions, time would accrue to the employee; if the employee only arranged/coordinated without participating, time would accrue only to the client.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether roundtable discussions coordinated by J. Lewis Associates for exchanging information between corporate and government officials would constitute 'direct communication' under lobbying regulations, and whether the company or its employees would be considered engaged in direct communication.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Roundtable meetings focused on information exchange without discussing pending or proposed legislation do not constitute direct communication. However, if legislation is proposed as a result of discussions, time spent counts as direct communication, with allocation depending on whether J. Lewis employees actively participate or only arrange the event.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains both the original incoming request (dated August 27, 1982) and FPPC response letter (dated October 21, 1982). Clear question and conclusion sections present. OCR quality appears good with minimal errors.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to J. Lewis Associates' inquiry about whether coordinating roundtable discussions between corporate and government officials would constitute lobbying requiring registration. The Commission concludes that information-exchange meetings without pending legislation discussion are not direct communication, but advises that if legislation results from discussions, time spent counts toward lobbying thresholds depending on employee participation level.
